behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is stateful retry sequencing (attempt counter + cap) across calls, not just a pure math helper. A unit test of backoff math can pass while integration between retry state and scheduler is wrong.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the retry wrapper with a stub transport that returns rate-limit failures for N attempts, then success/final failure. Use fake time to assert scheduled delays are 1x, 2x, 4x... and capped at max. Verify delay progression is based on consecutive failures in the same call lifecycle.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on response-shape parsing plus retry policy wiring (e.g., status code/headers/body mapping). Pure mocks of policy alone can miss misclassification bugs at module boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Run the client against a stubbed transport returning realistic HTTP-like rate-limit responses (e.g., 429 and Retry-After variants). Assert the retrier classifies as quota-limited and computes the next wait from response metadata/fallback policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This is cross-module async behavior: timer/backoff + request replay + response propagation. Unit tests can miss request reconstruction or promise-chain wiring errors.
behaviors.test_description: Use a stub transport that first returns rate-limited, then success. Assert the second attempt is sent with the same method/path/payload/headers as the original call and that `call()` resolves with the final success payload without exposing retry internals.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Type and error mapping must be validated at the public API boundary after real retry exhaustion, not only by unit-testing an error class.
behaviors.test_description: Configure max retries to a small number; stub transport to always return rate-limit responses. Assert `call()` rejects with `QuotaExhaustedError` (instance/type/code fields) and not generic/network error types.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Real risk is emergent from composed retry loop + quota state transitions. A unit test of any single function can miss non-terminating control-flow across modules.
behaviors.test_description: Drive a full call with controlled repeated rate-limit responses and bounded retries. Use fake timers and an overall test timeout guard; assert exact attempt count, final terminal error, and no further scheduled retries after termination.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: This is shared-state concurrency across callers/modules. Unit tests of a single caller path can miss quota partitioning bugs or accidental per-instance buckets.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate clients/callers that should share account quota in one process. Launch concurrent `call()` operations against a deterministic stub transport/quota manager and assert aggregate consumption reflects one shared bucket (not per-caller independent limits).

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering is a queue/scheduler property under async contention. Unit tests of queue primitives alone can miss integration bugs with retry wake-up and dispatcher handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Submit multiple calls in known order while quota/backoff forces waiting. Advance controlled time and release capacity deterministically; assert dispatch/start order matches submission order (FIFO), including after retries re-enter the scheduler.
